A0:
**Recommendation: Start with REST. Reach for GraphQL only if you hit a specific, concrete problem it solves.**

For most mobile apps, REST is the better default. Here's the reasoning, and the cases where I'd flip.

## Why REST wins by default for mobile

**1. HTTP caching is free and mobile needs it most.**
Mobile clients live on flaky networks and battery budgets. REST rides on standard HTTP caching — `ETag`, `Cache-Control`, CDN edge caching, even OS-level URL caches (`URLCache` on iOS, OkHttp cache on Android) work with zero extra code. GraphQL sends everything as `POST` to a single `/graphql` endpoint, so you lose all of that and have to rebuild caching at the client (normalized cache like Apollo/Relay) and can't lean on a CDN nearly as easily.

**2. Operational simplicity.**
REST is trivial to log, monitor, rate-limit per-endpoint, debug in a proxy (Charles/Proxyman), and reason about. A GraphQL endpoint is opaque to most of that tooling — one URL, variable-cost queries. You also inherit a real class of problems: query-depth/complexity attacks, N+1 resolver issues, and harder per-field authorization.

**3. Smaller surface, fewer moving parts.**
You don't need a schema layer, resolvers, codegen, or a client cache framework. For a small/medium team shipping an app, that's meaningfully less to build and maintain.

## Where GraphQL genuinely earns its place

Flip to GraphQL if **two or more** of these are true:

- **Many heterogeneous screens with very different data shapes**, and you're suffering real over-fetching/under-fetching — the classic "mobile makes 6 round trips to render one screen" problem. GraphQL lets the client fetch exactly one tailored payload per screen.
- **Multiple client types** (iOS, Android, web, watch, partners) all hitting the same backend with divergent needs. A typed schema as the single contract pays off at this fan-out.
- **A federated/microservices backend** where one screen aggregates many services — GraphQL (especially federation) is a nice aggregation layer.
- **Rapid, client-driven product iteration** where you want frontend teams to add fields without waiting on new backend endpoints each time.

If you mostly have one app, a handful of screens, and a single backend — none of that applies yet.

## The pragmatic middle path

If your pain is purely over-fetching on a few key screens, you don't need GraphQL — use **REST with tailored "view" endpoints** (e.g. `GET /home-feed` that returns exactly what the home screen needs) or sparse fieldsets (`?fields=...`). This solves 80% of the GraphQL motivation without the operational cost.

## Bottom line

| | Pick this if… |
|---|---|
| **REST** | Single app + single backend, you want caching/tooling/simplicity, small-to-mid team. **(default)** |
| **GraphQL** | Many clients or many backend services, real over-fetch pain across many divergent screens, frontend-driven iteration. |

Start REST, design clean resource endpoints, and only introduce GraphQL when you can name the specific screen or workflow that's hurting — not preemptively. Migrating REST→GraphQL later is very doable; you rarely regret starting simple.

If you tell me your stack (single app vs. multi-client, monolith vs. microservices, team size), I'll give you a firm one-way call instead of a framework.
A1s:
Default to **REST**, and reach for GraphQL only when you hit a specific set of conditions that REST handles poorly. For a typical mobile app, REST gets you to production faster with less operational risk, and the problems GraphQL solves usually don't bite until you're at meaningful scale or screen complexity.

Here's the reasoning, weighted for mobile specifically.

**Why REST as the default**

- **HTTP caching is free and mobile cares a lot about it.** REST resources map cleanly onto HTTP caching, CDNs, and `ETag`/`Cache-Control`. On flaky, high-latency mobile networks, cacheable GETs and offline-friendly caching are a real battery-and-data win. GraphQL POSTs a query to a single endpoint, so you lose standard HTTP caching and have to rebuild it client-side (e.g. Apollo's normalized cache), which is more moving parts.
- **Lower operational complexity.** Per-endpoint monitoring, rate limiting, auth scoping, and logging all work with tools you already have. GraphQL needs query-depth/complexity limiting, persisted queries, and N+1 protection (DataLoader) before it's safe in production — that's real work that's easy to under-invest in.
- **It's boring and well-understood.** Easier hiring, debugging, and third-party integration. Most teams ship correct REST faster than correct GraphQL.

**When GraphQL is the better call**

GraphQL was literally built at Facebook to solve *mobile* pain, so don't dismiss it. Pick it if several of these are true:

- Your screens are **heterogeneous and data-hungry** — each view needs a different slice stitched from multiple backend resources. GraphQL fetches exactly that in one round trip, avoiding the over-fetching and chatty multi-request waterfalls that hurt on mobile networks. (With REST you'd otherwise build custom aggregate/BFF endpoints anyway.)
- You support **multiple clients** (iOS, Android, web) that each want different shapes of the same data, and you don't want a backend change for every UI tweak. GraphQL lets the client drive the query.
- You have a **dedicated team** that will actually run the schema, codegen typed clients, and enforce the safety controls above.

**The mobile-versioning angle** cuts both ways: old app versions linger in the wild because users don't update. GraphQL's field-deprecation model handles gradual evolution well; REST typically needs explicit `/v2` versioning. Slight edge to GraphQL here, but it's manageable in REST.

My concrete suggestion: start with REST. If you find yourself building lots of bespoke "give me exactly this screen's data" aggregate endpoints, or juggling several clients with diverging data needs, that's your signal to introduce GraphQL — either wholesale or as a gateway in front of existing REST services.

If you tell me your team size, the number of client platforms, and how variable your screens are, I can turn this into a firm one-way recommendation rather than a default-with-conditions.

For a typical mobile app, REST gets you to production faster with less operational risk, and the problems GraphQL solves usually don't bite until you're at meaningful scale or screen complexity.

Here's the reasoning, weighted for mobile specifically.

**Why REST as the default**

- **HTTP caching is free and mobile cares a lot about it.** REST resources map cleanly onto HTTP caching, CDNs, and `ETag`/`Cache-Control`. On flaky, high-latency mobile networks, cacheable GETs and offline-friendly caching are a real battery-and-data win. GraphQL POSTs a query to a single endpoint, so you lose standard HTTP caching and have to rebuild it client-side (e.g. Apollo's normalized cache), which is more moving parts.
- **Lower operational complexity.** Per-endpoint monitoring, rate limiting, auth scoping, and logging all work with tools you already have. GraphQL needs query-depth/complexity limiting, persisted queries, and N+1 protection (DataLoader) before it's safe in production — that's real work that's easy to under-invest in.
- **It's boring and well-understood.** Easier hiring, debugging, and third-party integration. Most teams ship correct REST faster than correct GraphQL.

**When GraphQL is the better call**

GraphQL was literally built at Facebook to solve *mobile* pain, so don't dismiss it. Pick it if several of these are true:

- Your screens are **heterogeneous and data-hungry** — each view needs a different slice stitched from multiple backend resources. GraphQL fetches exactly that in one round trip, avoiding the over-fetching and chatty multi-request waterfalls that hurt on mobile networks. (With REST you'd otherwise build custom aggregate/BFF endpoints anyway.)
- You support **multiple clients** (iOS, Android, web) that each want different shapes of the same data, and you don't want a backend change for every UI tweak. GraphQL lets the client drive the query.
- You have a **dedicated team** that will actually run the schema, codegen typed clients, and enforce the safety controls above.

**The mobile-versioning angle** cuts both ways: old app versions linger in the wild because users don't update. GraphQL's field-deprecation model handles gradual evolution well; REST typically needs explicit `/v2` versioning. Slight edge to GraphQL here, but it's manageable in REST.

My concrete suggestion: start with REST. If you find yourself building lots of bespoke "give me exactly this screen's data" aggregate endpoints, or juggling several clients with diverging data needs, that's your signal to introduce GraphQL — either wholesale or as a gateway in front of existing REST services.

**What would flip this recommendation:** if you already know your app launches with three-plus client platforms consuming divergent data shapes, your screens are highly composite from day one, and you have a team that's run GraphQL in production before, then "start with REST" is the wrong call — go GraphQL up front, because you'd otherwise spend the first few months reinventing it as BFF endpoints. The key limitation of this advice is that it's a default for the *typical* case and leans on the assumption that you're early-stage with one or two clients and uncertain screen complexity; the more those assumptions are false, the weaker the REST default gets. It also doesn't weigh ecosystem lock-in (existing backend, team's current expertise, third-party APIs you must integrate), which can dominate the decision regardless of the technical fit above.

If you tell me your team size, the number of client platforms, and how variable your screens are, I can turn this into a firm one-way recommendation rather than a default-with-conditions.
